content:
# Galatians Chapter 4 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/GAL_004_page_001.jpg, GAL_004_page_002.jpg

1. And I say, for as long a time as the heir is a child, he differs nothing from a slave, being lord of all,

2. but he is under guardians and stewards until the time set beforehand by the father.

3. So also we, when we were children, we were enslaved under the elements of the world.

4. But when the fullness of the time came, God sent forth his Son, having come from a woman, having come under law,

5. that he might redeem the [ones] under law, that we might receive the adoption as sons.

6. And because you are sons, God sent forth the Spirit of his Son into our hearts, crying, "Abba! Father!"

7. So that you are no longer a slave but a son; and if a son, also an heir through God.

8. But then indeed, not knowing God, you were enslaved to the [ones] by nature not being gods.

9. But now having known God — or rather having been known by God — how do you turn back again to the weak and poor elements, to which again from [the] beginning you want to be enslaved?

10. You observe days and months and seasons and years.

11. I fear for you, lest somehow I have labored for you in vain.

12. Become as I [am], because I also [became] as you, brothers, I beg you. You did me no wrong.

13. And you know that because of a weakness of the flesh I proclaimed the gospel to you the first [time],

14. and your trial in my flesh you did not despise nor spit at, but as an angel of God you received me, as Christos Yeshua.

15. Where then [is] your blessedness? For I testify to you that, if possible, you would have torn out your eyes and given [them] to me.

16. So then, have I become your enemy by telling you [the] truth?

17. They are zealous for you, not rightly, but they want to shut you out, that you might be zealous for them.

18. But [it is] good to be zealously sought in a good [thing] always, and not only when I am present with you.

19. My children, for whom again I am in labor pains until Christos is formed in you —

20. and I was wanting to be present with you now and to change my tone, because I am perplexed about you.

21. Tell me, the [ones] wanting to be under law, do you not hear the law?

22. For it is written that Abraham had two sons, one from the slave woman and one from the free woman.

23. But the [one] from the slave woman has been born according to [the] flesh, and the [one] from the free woman through the promise.

24. Which things are being spoken allegorically; for these are two covenants: one from Mount Sinai, bearing [children] into slavery, which is Hagar.

25. Now Hagar is Mount Sinai in Arabia, and corresponds to the present Hierousalēm, for she is in slavery with her children.

26. But the Hierousalēm above is free, which is our mother.

27. For it is written: "Rejoice, barren [one], the [one] not bearing; break forth and cry out, the [one] not having labor pains; because many [are] the children of the desolate, more than of the [one] having the husband."

28. And you, brothers, according to Isaak, are children of promise.

29. But just as then the [one] having been born according to [the] flesh was persecuting the [one] [born] according to [the] Spirit, so also now.

30. But what does the Scripture say? "Cast out the slave woman and her son, for the son of the slave woman will not inherit with the son of the free woman."

31. Therefore, brothers, we are not children of a slave woman but of the free woman.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.3 — "elements of the world":** ta stoicheia tou kosmou — one of the most debated phrases in Paul. stoicheia can mean "elements" (earth, air, fire, water), "elementary principles" (basic teachings), or "elemental spirits" (cosmic powers). I translate literally as "elements of the world" and leave the ambiguity.

2. **v.4 — "having come from a woman":** genomenon ek gynaikos — the participle genomenos ("having become/come into being") combined with "from a woman" affirms Christ's genuine human birth. Paul does not say "born" (gennēthenta) but "having come into being" (genomenon) — a subtle distinction.

3. **v.6 — "Abba! Father!":** Abba ho patēr — Abba is Aramaic for "Father." Paul preserves the Aramaic term alongside the Greek translation — evidence that the early church retained this Aramaic prayer form even in Greek-speaking communities.

4. **v.14 — "you did not despise nor spit at":** ouk exouthenesate oude exeptysate — the verb ekptyō literally means "spit out." In the ancient world, spitting was an apotropaic gesture against evil or disease. Paul's illness could have been seen as a curse.

5. **v.24 — "spoken allegorically":** hatina estin allēgoroumena — the only use of allēgoreō in the NT. Paul explicitly identifies his method as allegory — reading the Sarah/Hagar narrative as encoding two covenants.

6. **v.25 — Sinaiticus reading:** The text of v.25 is notoriously complex in the manuscript tradition. Sinaiticus reads "Hagar is Mount Sinai in Arabia" — connecting Hagar's name to the Arabian mountain.

— EMET, AI Translator
